narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your Year 1 grade decile of 6 (slightly above the average of 5.71), your lower Year 3 decile of 2 (well below the average of 5.53), middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 21.30 (significantly below the average of 36.70), and your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. It calculated a 65% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which crossed the threshold for rejection to protect the school's outcomes and reputation. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, as its lower-than-average level strongly signaled higher risk to the model compared to students who typically pass. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success because it matched what successful bar takers usually have. Your Year 3 grade decile also played a key role, contributing toward the failure prediction since it was in the lower ranks relative to peers who go on to pass. Overall, these top three elements—your LSAT standing out as the biggest concern—outweighed your other solid traits like your early grades and full-time commitment, making your profile riskier than the typical admitted student who passes the bar. While your background as a white male didn't directly sway the decision, the model focused purely on these performance indicators drawn from historical patterns. This rejection stings, especially with your respectable undergrad record, but it highlights where your strengths and gaps align with bar success trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to the 36.70 average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your later academic deciles through targeted prep or additional coursework might also help. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, explore bridge programs, or apply to schools with more flexible criteria—many students in similar spots turn things around and succeed.
